title: Președintele Bulgariei a demisionat pentru a intra în arena politicii „murdare”. Ce urmează în țara vecină?
summary: Bulgarian President Rumen Radev resigned in January 2026 to enter active politics, leaving the country in an institutional vacuum while it paradoxically achieves strategic goals like eurozone accession. His departure triggered intense polarization, with supporters seeing him as a patriotic reformer and critics viewing him as another oligarchic figure disguised in anti-corruption rhetoric.
talking_points: Bulgaria operates on 'autopilot' without an approved budget, with a caretaker government, and key institutional pillars either decapitated or delegitimized
Radev's resignation discourse centers on a 'new social contract' and criticism of 'facade democracy' where oligarchic structures control real power outside official institutions
Radev personalizes the oligarchic model around Delian Peevski while himself maintaining ties to influential business circles and security establishment
The anti-corruption rhetoric aligns with urban middle-class parties but Radev's motivations appear rooted in deep state factional disputes rather than genuine institutional reform
Bulgaria's institutional crisis coexists with achieving strategic objectives like Schengen and eurozone entry, creating an ironic sense that the state functions through inertia
author: Vladimir Mitev
